votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the string length check and iterates over all valid indices to ensure every 3 consecutive characters are distinct, exactly matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking that the string length is at least 3 and that all adjacent triples of characters are distinct.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking that the string length is at least 3 and that every sliding window of 3 characters contains distinct characters.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. nth_error_string returns the character at index n or None if out of bounds. distinct3 checks all three characters are pairwise distinct. happy_prop checks length >= 3 and that every consecutive triple (at positions i, i+1, i+2 where i+2 < len(s)) has distinct characters. The range(len(s) - 2) correctly iterates i from 0 to len(s)-3, matching the Coq condition i + 2 < length. is_happy_spec returns true iff r matches the boolean result of happy_prop, which correctly captures the Coq specification that r=true iff happy_prop holds.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The happy_prop function checks that the string has length >= 3 and that every consecutive triple of characters (at positions i, i+1, i+2 where i+2 < len(s)) are all distinct. The is_happy_spec function returns true when r matches the boolean result of happy_prop, which corresponds to the Coq spec that says r=true iff happy_prop holds and r=false iff happy_prop doesn't hold. The range(len(s) - 2) correctly iterates over all valid i values where i + 2 < len(s).

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The happy_prop function checks that the string has length >= 3 and that every consecutive triple of characters (at positions i, i+1, i+2 where i+2 < len(s)) are all distinct. The is_happy_spec function returns true when r matches the boolean result of happy_prop, which corresponds to the Coq spec's disjunction that r=true iff happy_prop holds and r=false iff happy_prop doesn't hold. The range(len(s) - 2) correctly iterates over all valid i values where i + 2 < len(s).

Both define happy as length >= 3 and every consecutive triple having pairwise distinct characters; the loop ranges and indexing match Coq’s nth_error behavior, and is_happy_spec returns r == happy_prop(s).

Both define nth_error_string as in-bounds index lookup (None otherwise), happy_prop as length>=3 with all consecutive triples pairwise distinct, and is_happy_spec as r matching happy_prop(s). Indices and bounds handling align, so results match on all concrete (ASCII) strings.

Both define happiness as length >= 3 and every i..i+2 window having pairwise distinct chars; Python’s indexing and checks match Coq’s nth_error and quantification, and is_happy_spec returns r equals that property.
